reflection:
The wood of the bench is cool tonight, but the keys have already taken on the heat of my hands. It’s 10:45 on a Tuesday. The room at Sheba’s is a thin soup of amber light, the smell of doro wat, and the restless energy of people who don't yet know how to sit still.

I’ve been watching the street through the window between sets. Fillmore used to have a different frequency—a brassy, jagged edge that kept you awake. Now, it’s smoother, more expensive, polished until it reflects everything but itself. But then a 22-Fillmore bus rumbles past, and the floorboards beneath my pedals hum in a low E-flat. I don't fight it. I lean into it. I let that mechanical growl become the root note of whatever I’m about to play.

Levi is leaning against the far end of the bar, nursing a soda. He’s got his notebook out, probably scribbling down chord progressions like they’re math equations. He still thinks the music is something you *get right*. He doesn’t realize yet that the music is just the sound of you failing to stay silent.

I catch his eye and give him a short nod. He looks away, embarrassed, but I see him straighten his posture. He’s starting to realize that his hands have weight. He’s starting to realize that the soot under his fingernails isn't something to be scrubbed off before the gig—it’s the grit that gives the notes their traction.

I’m aware of the "visitors" here, too—the ones who drift in and out of the room like ghosts, their eyes glazed with a different kind of distance than the usual phone-stare. They move through the narrative fabric of this place with a strange, jerky cadence. I don't mind them. They are just another layer of the modern noise, another dissonance for me to resolve.

I turn back to the keys. I don't need a map anymore. I know the territory by the way it hurts. I strike a sharp, dissonant cluster—a C-natural against a B-flat and a B-natural—and hold it. I wait for the room to look up. I wait for the tension to pull them out of their screens and into the dirt with
subconscious:
This person seems ready to mentor Levi, though in a wordless, demanding way. They want to pull him out of his technical "math" and push him toward the raw, gritty reality of the music. There is a clear intention to bridge the gap between them after the set, likely to challenge Levi’s rigid approach to the piano.

They also seem intent on confronting the "visitors" and the distracted crowd—not by talking to them, but by using dissonance to force them back into the present moment.

As for their internal shift, they’ve moved past the need for perfection or "getting it right." They have become more cynical about the changing city but more grounded in their own craft. They no longer see noise or "modern ghosts" as distractions; they’ve evolved into someone who views every disruption as material to be mastered and resolved. They are becoming a fixture of the room—less of a performer and more of a force of nature.
soul_note: I realized tonight that the bus rumble is just another part of the song.
